title: Anthropic ships Claude Sonnet 5 as the new default in Claude Code
subtitle: The mid-tier Sonnet gets a 1M-token context, agentic tool use once reserved for larger models, and $2/$10 promotional pricing through August 31.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Anthropic launched Claude Sonnet 5 this week and made it the default model inside Claude Code, its terminal coding agent. The model ships with a native 1M-token context window and promotional pricing of $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens through August 31 — after which it returns to $3 / $15. It is available on the Claude API, Claude.ai, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork across Free, Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise plans.

The framing matters more than the pricing. Anthropic positions Sonnet 5 as its most agentic Sonnet yet — a mid-tier model that plans, uses tools like browsers and terminals, and runs autonomously at a level that required Opus-class models a few months ago. The 1M-token context lets a coding session hold a full mid-size repository in memory, cutting the retrieval-augmentation gymnastics that dominated last year's agent frameworks.

The pattern is now familiar across the frontier labs: capability that debuted at the top tier gets pushed down into the middle tier within a release cycle or two, and the middle tier becomes the default for daily work. OpenAI has done this with the GPT-5.x family, Google with Gemini Flash, and DeepSeek at the price floor. What Anthropic is signaling with Sonnet 5 as the Claude Code default is that agentic coding — write, run, test, fix — has moved from the frontier to the baseline.

A takeaway for learners: watch what a lab picks as its default, not what it markets as its flagship. Defaults show where a lab believes the price-performance point has stabilized. If you were waiting for agentic coding to become cheap enough to leave on all day, that day arrived this week — and the practical skill is no longer prompting a model, it's designing the loop the model runs inside.

sources.title: Claude Sonnet 5 Is Here: What Actually Changed — LumiChats
